_meta.notes: Rust-Hand-faction ascended form (default). The Rust Hand traffics in iron's slow inevitability. The Ascendant has stopped fighting the rust and started conducting it.

tagline: You stopped fighting the rust. You started conducting it.

negativeVocab.rule: Rust Hand Ascendants do not gleam. Vocabulary of polish does not fit.

hardMoves.rule: Partial-success: pick one. The Ascendant's costs land in iron going wrong slowly.

moves.narrative: A join you forged today will fail in a year. You can already see it.
The iron took the rust at the wrong angle. You let it.
A tool that has served you many years will not see another season.
A rust-mark on your own forearm has spread.
An iron blank sings under your hammer in a frequency you did not strike. The Long Wyrm has hummed under your forge.

success: You lay a mark on the iron the way the rust will read it. The mark settles in cleanly; the patina, in a year, will know where to go.
Your mark is precise. The piece will weather along the lines you asked of it.
You mark. The line is the line your master would have laid; the rust will follow it like water finding the gradient.
Your mark settles into the iron at exactly the depth that lets the patina take but not the rot.
partial: You mark. The mark holds; one corner will read it differently than you intended.
Your marking lands. The angle is a degree off and the year will show it.
You mark. The line is correct in shape; the depth varies by a hair across its length.
Your mark holds. A small section will weather faster than the rest; the customer may notice in three years.
miss: You mark, and the iron rejects the line. The patina will not start where you wanted.
Your mark fades faster than your hand has left the metal. You will redo it tomorrow.
You mark. The iron has a hidden grain that runs against your line; the mark will fight the patina's gradient.
Your line wanders. The rasp slipped twice; the mark is the wrong mark for this piece.

success: You leave the piece to weather. Time is the third tool, after hammer and patience.
Your weathering is correct. The piece, three months from now, will look exactly as you have predicted.
You set the piece on the south rack. The wind off the Furrow takes the edge; the rust takes the rest.
Your weathering reads true. By autumn the patina will have closed the surface against further damage.
partial: You weather. A line of frost crossed the piece during the night that you did not anticipate.
Your weathering holds. A patch of patina has come up brighter than the rest.
You weather. A bird left a streak across the upper surface; the patina will absorb it but the trace will show.
Your weathering is correct. The customer will need to oil one corner that took the season harder than the rest.
miss: The piece weathers wrong. Whatever the air did to it overnight, it was not what you asked.
Your weathering fails. The rust has refused the surface; the patina will not take this season.
You weather. A wet week settles in; the rust comes too fast and too deep. The piece is past saving.
Your weathering plan is upset by a frost three weeks early; the patina cracks where it should have settled.

success: You forge the join. The two pieces lock; the seam is where you want it; the rust, when it comes, will follow the seam true.
Your join is sound. It will outlive the customer's grandchild.
You join. The seam takes on the third blow; the metal accepts the union as if it had always been one piece.
Your seam closes clean. The hammer-line is invisible by the time the patina has had a year.
partial: You join. The seam is correct. The seam will fail in seventeen years; you can already see when.
Your join holds. A small misalignment will show under hard use.
You join. The seam closes; one micro-gap at the seam's middle will admit moisture in a way you didn't catch.
Your join is honest. It will hold for the customer's lifetime, but the seam's signature will be visible for the patina-reader.
miss: You join. The pieces hold for now. They will not hold for long.
Your seam refuses to seat. You will reforge it.
You join. The two pieces resist each other; the seam is two seams instead of one and will fail at the join.
Your join is wrong. You knock the piece back into the bin and start the second pass.

success: You set down the rasp and rest. The forge cools; the piece on the rack continues without you.
Your rest is full. The iron's slow work continues; you have only paused yours.
You rest. The patina you set yesterday continues its quiet progress; you do not have to watch it.
Your rest is honest. The piece will be there in the morning; so will you.
partial: You rest. The forge banked unevenly; you will tend it before sleep.
Your rest holds. A piece is humming with leftover patience that you almost missed.
You rest. The wind off the Furrow has shifted; one piece on the rack will need turning.
Your rest closes. A fly has settled on a fresh-marked piece; the mark will have a small flaw.
miss: You try to rest. The customer's piece is wrong, and you cannot stop knowing it.
Your rest fails. The forge has cooled past where it ought to have.
You try to rest. A piece on the rack is making the wrong sound; you'll be up in an hour.
Your rest declines. Tomorrow's joining is in your shoulders already, refusing to wait.

success: An iron blank sings under your hammer in a frequency you did not strike. The Long Wyrm has hummed under your forge.
The piece you finish today carries a patina you have not put there. The Wyrm has marked the iron.
partial: The Wyrm-note enters. The Wyrm-note also draws something across the workshop you cannot see.
Your iron rings older than your craft. The piece is changed; the customer will sense the change without naming it.
miss: You strike, and the iron rings only with your work. The Wyrm is not at the forge today.
Your hammer is your hammer. The piece is your piece.

success: You hold the work. The iron remembers what you have asked of it.
partial: You wait. A patch of rust has chosen a corner you did not want it to.
miss: You wait. The forge has cooled, and the customer's piece will be late.

opening: The piece has come back. The customer who took it home a year ago has brought it for the first inspection. You turn it in your hand. The mark you laid on the head has settled correctly; the rust has begun where you wanted it to begin and not where you didn't. You give the customer a single nod. They are pleased.

firstGoal.label: mark the iron
firstGoal.description: Lay marks through three turns. The rust will follow what you've decided.

sampleCorpus.rule: Rust-Hand prose is unhurried, attentive to slow change, comfortable with imperfection.

passages.scenario: The Rust Hand Ascendant inspects a forge piece a year after forging it
passages.text:
The piece has come back. The customer who took it home a year ago has brought it for the first inspection.

You turn it in your hand. The mark you laid on the head has settled correctly; the rust has begun where you wanted it to begin and not where you didn't. You give the customer a single nod. They are pleased; they will be more pleased in another year, when the mark will deepen into the patina you predicted.

The rust is doing what you asked it to do. This took a long time to learn. Now it is yours.
